content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Status quo plus incremental adaptation'. Closing small-village primary schools in depopulating Hungarian settlements is not justified as an efficiency measure on current evidence, because per-pupil cost premiums are economically defensible, learning outcomes do not suffer materially, and closure-induced local population loss and community harm systematically outweigh accounting savings.
opportunities: Preserves the school's documented community-hub and place-identity function without requiring new legal or institutional structures.
Avoids abrupt travel-burden increases for families, since children continue attending the local school rather than being reassigned under mandatory catchment rules.
strengths: Hungary's primary education school-site network declined from 3,609 sites (2021) to 3,521 sites (2024), while over 1,200 of 3,155 settlements now have no public educational institution.
Hungary's annual births fell 22.5% in ten years (110,800 in 2015 to 77,600 in 2025), with primary-grade enrollment projected to shrink from ~110,000 to ~72,000 annually by 2030.
threats: Chronic under-budgeting of the district (tankerület) system creates persistent fiscal pressure to consolidate regardless of stated 'protect as long as there is demand' policy, risking quiet erosion rather than open confrontation.
Absence of a bright-line statutory threshold leaves retention decisions to discretionary tankerület judgment, which is politically reversible year to year and vulnerable to administration change.
weaknesses: The magnitude and timeline of actual primary-grade enrollment loss in rural versus urban areas remain uncertain; district-level projections are scarce.

The actual cost differential between small-village school operation and consolidated/bussed alternatives (including all transportation, facilities, teacher deployment, and psychosocial costs) has not been reliably estimated for Hungary.
